description_long: This guard stone is a roughly hewn, upright block of pale stone with a pointed top and irregular, faceted sides. Its surface is predominantly a mottled white and light gray, with scattered darker speckles and patches that suggest natural mineral inclusions or weathering. The stone appears dense and solid, with a slightly glossy sheen in some areas, possibly from years of exposure to the elements. The overall shape is polygonal and somewhat conical, tapering to a blunt point at the top, while the base is broader and more stable. The texture is uneven, with visible roughness and minor pitting, indicating it has not been highly polished. This object is typical of guard stones used to protect corners, gateways, or pedestrian areas from vehicle encroachment in urban environments.
description: A pale, pointed, roughly hewn stone block with a mottled white and gray surface, featuring a conical top and irregular, faceted sides. Its dense, solid appearance and rough texture suggest durability and weather resistance.
description_view_0: The front view shows the pointed top and broad base, with the most prominent faceting and mottled surface details visible.
description_view_1: This side view highlights the elongated, slightly curved profile and the irregular, rough surface texture.
description_view_2: The rear view displays the conical taper and more subtle surface mottling, with less pronounced faceting.
description_view_3: This side view reveals a smoother, less angular surface with some visible pitting and a gentle curve along the height.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the edge of urban sidewalks, at the corners of building entrances, or at the entrance to driveways and alleyways to prevent vehicle encroachment and protect structures.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be installed upright, with the base partially embedded in the ground for stability and the pointed end facing upward.
attribute_wear_patterns: Likely to show chipping and smoothing at the edges and corners from vehicle contact, as well as surface discoloration and minor erosion from weather exposure. The base may exhibit dirt accumulation and abrasion from foot traffic and street debris.
